questions.prompt: Which tooth is the broader, squarer anterior reference with the sharper mesioincisal corner?
questions.explanation: The maxillary central incisor is the widest anterior crown and typically keeps a sharper mesioincisal corner. The lateral is the reduced, rounder, more variable neighbor.
questions.distractorExplanation: The maxillary lateral incisor is smaller and rounder, especially at the distal corner; its variability makes it a poor choice for the broad, square reference form.

comparisonRows.label: Crown shape
comparisonRows.toothA: Broad, relatively square crown; sharp mesioincisal corner
comparisonRows.toothB: Smaller, rounder crown; distal corner especially rounded

comparisonRows.label: Lingual anatomy
comparisonRows.toothA: Moderate fossa and ridges; cingulum commonly distal
comparisonRows.toothB: Deeper or more expressed lingual form; cingulum more centered

comparisonRows.label: Root pattern
comparisonRows.toothA: Single conical root; mesial surface may be flat or depressed
comparisonRows.toothB: Single, more slender root; mesial depression may be present

comparisonRows.label: Ridge/groove clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Moderate marginal ridges around one lingual fossa
comparisonRows.toothB: More variable lingual pits or grooves may occur

comparisonRows.label: Arch clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Broad anterior anchor nearest the midline
comparisonRows.toothB: Reduced, rounder neighbor between central and canine

comparisonRows.label: Clinical identification clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Use crown width plus the sharp mesioincisal corner
comparisonRows.toothB: Use smaller size, rounded corners, and variability

questions.commonTrap: Do not identify the lateral from roundness alone; confirm its smaller crown and stronger lingual variability.

questions.prompt: Which tooth more commonly combines a centered cingulum with deeper, more variable lingual anatomy?
questions.explanation: The maxillary lateral incisor typically has a more centered cingulum and more pronounced or variable lingual form than the central, including possible pits and grooves.
questions.distractorExplanation: The maxillary central usually has moderate lingual anatomy and a cingulum that trends distal rather than centered.

comparisonRows.label: Crown shape
comparisonRows.toothA: Broad, relatively square crown; sharp mesioincisal corner
comparisonRows.toothB: Smaller, rounder crown; distal corner especially rounded

comparisonRows.label: Lingual anatomy
comparisonRows.toothA: Moderate fossa and ridges; cingulum commonly distal
comparisonRows.toothB: Deeper or more expressed lingual form; cingulum more centered

comparisonRows.label: Root pattern
comparisonRows.toothA: Single conical root; mesial surface may be flat or depressed
comparisonRows.toothB: Single, more slender root; mesial depression may be present

comparisonRows.label: Ridge/groove clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Moderate marginal ridges around one lingual fossa
comparisonRows.toothB: More variable lingual pits or grooves may occur

comparisonRows.label: Arch clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Broad anterior anchor nearest the midline
comparisonRows.toothB: Reduced, rounder neighbor between central and canine

comparisonRows.label: Clinical identification clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Use crown width plus the sharp mesioincisal corner
comparisonRows.toothB: Use smaller size, rounded corners, and variability

questions.commonTrap: A maxillary lateral can be highly variable. Treat a pit or groove as supporting evidence, not a required feature.

questions.prompt: Which tooth is the smallest permanent tooth and the more bilaterally symmetrical member of this pair?
questions.explanation: The mandibular central incisor is the smallest permanent tooth and is notably symmetrical from facial, lingual, and incisal views.
questions.distractorExplanation: The mandibular lateral is slightly larger and loses bilateral symmetry as its distal incisal edge twists to follow the arch.

comparisonRows.label: Crown shape
comparisonRows.toothA: Narrowest crown; straight incisal edge and sharp corners
comparisonRows.toothB: Slightly larger; distal corner and incisal edge are less regular

comparisonRows.label: Lingual anatomy
comparisonRows.toothA: Very shallow fossa; indistinct ridges; centered cingulum
comparisonRows.toothB: Shallow fossa; cingulum commonly distal

comparisonRows.label: Root pattern
comparisonRows.toothA: Single root with mesial and usually deeper distal depression
comparisonRows.toothB: Similar single-root depression pattern; root alone is a weak separator

comparisonRows.label: Ridge/groove clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Least pronounced incisor marginal ridges
comparisonRows.toothB: Mesial marginal ridge commonly longer than distal

comparisonRows.label: Arch clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Straight edge bisects the crown
comparisonRows.toothB: Distal twist follows the curvature of the arch

comparisonRows.label: Clinical identification clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Small size plus bilateral symmetry
comparisonRows.toothB: Slightly larger crown plus distal twist

questions.commonTrap: Both teeth have weak lingual anatomy. Symmetry and distal twist are more reliable than fossa depth.

sourceRefs.sourceName: REHE 151 Dental Anatomy Course Mastery Guide.pdf
REHE 151 Dental Anatomy and Occlusion Textbook Companion.pdf
REHE 151 - mand ci 9 2 21final.txt

questions.prompt: Which tooth uses a distal incisal twist as the reliable clue that it follows the dental arch?
questions.explanation: The mandibular lateral incisor twists distally from the incisal view. That asymmetry is the fastest separator from the central incisor.
questions.distractorExplanation: The mandibular central keeps a straighter incisal edge and much stronger bilateral symmetry; it does not supply the characteristic distal arch twist.

comparisonRows.label: Crown shape
comparisonRows.toothA: Narrowest crown; straight incisal edge and sharp corners
comparisonRows.toothB: Slightly larger; distal corner and incisal edge are less regular

comparisonRows.label: Lingual anatomy
comparisonRows.toothA: Very shallow fossa; indistinct ridges; centered cingulum
comparisonRows.toothB: Shallow fossa; cingulum commonly distal

comparisonRows.label: Root pattern
comparisonRows.toothA: Single root with mesial and usually deeper distal depression
comparisonRows.toothB: Similar single-root depression pattern; root alone is a weak separator

comparisonRows.label: Ridge/groove clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Least pronounced incisor marginal ridges
comparisonRows.toothB: Mesial marginal ridge commonly longer than distal

comparisonRows.label: Arch clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Straight edge bisects the crown
comparisonRows.toothB: Distal twist follows the curvature of the arch

comparisonRows.label: Clinical identification clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Small size plus bilateral symmetry
comparisonRows.toothB: Slightly larger crown plus distal twist

questions.commonTrap: Wear can obscure identification clues generally. Confirm the distal twist with the distally positioned cingulum and slightly larger crown instead of relying on one edge alone.

questions.prompt: Which canine has the stronger lingual ridge, two clearer fossae, and the larger centered cingulum?
questions.explanation: The maxillary canine is the bulkier canine with a prominent lingual ridge, two fossae, and a larger centered cingulum.
questions.distractorExplanation: The mandibular canine has shallower lingual anatomy and a smaller cingulum that may sit distal to center.

comparisonRows.label: Crown shape
comparisonRows.toothA: Bulkier diamond form with a characteristic distal pinch
comparisonRows.toothB: Longer, narrower crown with less facial bulk

comparisonRows.label: Lingual anatomy
comparisonRows.toothA: Prominent lingual ridge, two fossae, large centered cingulum
comparisonRows.toothB: Shallower fossae, weaker ridge, smaller distal or central cingulum

comparisonRows.label: Root pattern
comparisonRows.toothA: Very long, thick single-root expectation
comparisonRows.toothB: Long root; anterior tooth most likely to show two roots or canals

comparisonRows.label: Ridge/groove clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Prominent facial ridge with depressions on each side
comparisonRows.toothB: Facial and lingual ridges less pronounced

comparisonRows.label: Arch clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Cusp tip sits more facial to the root axis
comparisonRows.toothB: Cusp tip sits more lingual; distal arm follows arch form

comparisonRows.label: Clinical identification clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Canine eminence, bulk, and distal pinch
comparisonRows.toothB: Slender crown plus possible two-root or two-canal variation

questions.commonTrap: Do not call every pointed tooth a maxillary canine. Check ridge strength, cingulum size, and cusp-tip position.

sourceRefs.sourceName: REHE 151 Dental Anatomy Course Mastery Guide.pdf
REHE 151 Dental Anatomy and Occlusion Textbook Companion.pdf

questions.prompt: Which canine should raise the stronger possibility of two roots or two canals rather than a guaranteed single canal?
questions.explanation: The mandibular canine is taught as the anterior tooth most likely to show two roots or two canals. That is a variation cue, not an absolute anatomy claim.
questions.distractorExplanation: The maxillary canine is the classic long, thick, stable root landmark; it is not the stronger two-root or two-canal variation cue in this comparison.

comparisonRows.label: Crown shape
comparisonRows.toothA: Bulkier diamond form with a characteristic distal pinch
comparisonRows.toothB: Longer, narrower crown with less facial bulk

comparisonRows.label: Lingual anatomy
comparisonRows.toothA: Prominent lingual ridge, two fossae, large centered cingulum
comparisonRows.toothB: Shallower fossae, weaker ridge, smaller distal or central cingulum

comparisonRows.label: Root pattern
comparisonRows.toothA: Very long, thick single-root expectation
comparisonRows.toothB: Long root; anterior tooth most likely to show two roots or canals

comparisonRows.label: Ridge/groove clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Prominent facial ridge with depressions on each side
comparisonRows.toothB: Facial and lingual ridges less pronounced

comparisonRows.label: Arch clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Cusp tip sits more facial to the root axis
comparisonRows.toothB: Cusp tip sits more lingual; distal arm follows arch form

comparisonRows.label: Clinical identification clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Canine eminence, bulk, and distal pinch
comparisonRows.toothB: Slender crown plus possible two-root or two-canal variation

questions.commonTrap: “Most likely anterior tooth” does not mean “usually two-rooted.” Preserve the possibility language.

sourceRefs.sourceName: REHE 151 Dental Anatomy Course Mastery Guide.pdf
REHE 151 Dental Anatomy and Occlusion Textbook Companion.pdf
da 3 Mand C 2024.ppt

questions.prompt: Which premolar is the stronger match for the common facial-lingual two-root variant and a pronounced mesial crown-root depression?
questions.explanation: The course deck reports two roots in 61% of maxillary first premolars (one root in 38% and three roots in 1%). Its common two-root variant is oriented facial-lingual, and the pronounced mesial coronal/root depression remains a key clue.
questions.distractorExplanation: The maxillary second premolar more commonly has one root and does not carry the first premolar’s defining mesial crown-root depression pattern.

comparisonRows.label: Crown shape
comparisonRows.toothA: Sharper, angular tapered hexagon
comparisonRows.toothB: Rounder, more even oval or rectangular form

comparisonRows.label: Lingual anatomy
comparisonRows.toothA: Lingual cusp is shorter; crown tapers more lingually
comparisonRows.toothB: Lingual cusp closer to facial cusp height; less lingual taper

comparisonRows.label: Root pattern
comparisonRows.toothA: Common F/L two-root variant (61%); one root 38%, three roots 1%
comparisonRows.toothB: Usually one root; distal depression is more typical

comparisonRows.label: Ridge/groove clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Classic mesial marginal groove and longer central groove
comparisonRows.toothB: Shorter central groove with more supplemental grooves

comparisonRows.label: Arch clue
comparisonRows.toothA: More angular and asymmetric first premolar
comparisonRows.toothB: More balanced, rounded second premolar

comparisonRows.label: Clinical identification clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Mesial crown-root depression plus bifurcation tendency
comparisonRows.toothB: One-root tendency plus rounded, wrinkled occlusal table

questions.commonTrap: Root count and canal count are related but not interchangeable; use “commonly” and confirm both separately.

sourceRefs.sourceName: REHE 151 Dental Anatomy Course Mastery Guide.pdf
REHE 151 Dental Anatomy and Occlusion Textbook Companion.pdf
da 5 Max PM1 2024.ppt
da 5 Max PM2 2024.ppt

questions.prompt: Which tooth is the more rounded, even premolar with a shorter central groove and more supplemental grooves?
questions.explanation: The maxillary second premolar is the more balanced, rounded tooth and commonly shows a shorter central groove with a more wrinkled supplemental-groove pattern.
questions.distractorExplanation: The maxillary first premolar is more angular and is better recognized by its longer central groove, classic mesial marginal groove, and mesial depression.

comparisonRows.label: Crown shape
comparisonRows.toothA: Sharper, angular tapered hexagon
comparisonRows.toothB: Rounder, more even oval or rectangular form

comparisonRows.label: Lingual anatomy
comparisonRows.toothA: Lingual cusp is shorter; crown tapers more lingually
comparisonRows.toothB: Lingual cusp closer to facial cusp height; less lingual taper

comparisonRows.label: Root pattern
comparisonRows.toothA: Commonly two roots or two canals; mesial depression is the key exception
comparisonRows.toothB: Usually one root; distal depression is more typical

comparisonRows.label: Ridge/groove clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Classic mesial marginal groove and longer central groove
comparisonRows.toothB: Shorter central groove with more supplemental grooves

comparisonRows.label: Arch clue
comparisonRows.toothA: More angular and asymmetric first premolar
comparisonRows.toothB: More balanced, rounded second premolar

comparisonRows.label: Clinical identification clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Mesial crown-root depression plus bifurcation tendency
comparisonRows.toothB: One-root tendency plus rounded, wrinkled occlusal table

questions.commonTrap: Do not use one groove alone. Confirm the second premolar with its shorter central groove, supplemental grooves, rounded outline, and one-root tendency.

sourceRefs.sourceName: REHE 151 Dental Anatomy Course Mastery Guide.pdf
REHE 151 Dental Anatomy and Occlusion Textbook Companion.pdf

da 5 Max PM2 2024.ppt

questions.prompt: Which premolar is the classic no-central-groove tooth with a dominant transverse ridge and tiny lingual cusp?
questions.explanation: The mandibular first premolar is dominated by the facial cusp and a strong transverse ridge; its classic occlusal pattern lacks a central groove and may show an ML groove.
questions.distractorExplanation: The mandibular second premolar has a more functional lingual cusp system and commonly uses H/U grooves in the two-cusp type or a Y pattern in the three-cusp type.

comparisonRows.label: Crown shape
comparisonRows.toothA: Diamond outline with triangular occlusal table
comparisonRows.toothB: Ovoid two-cusp or squarer three-cusp outline

comparisonRows.label: Lingual anatomy
comparisonRows.toothA: Tiny pointed, often nonfunctional lingual cusp
comparisonRows.toothB: Larger functional lingual cusp or ML and DL cusps

comparisonRows.label: Root pattern
comparisonRows.toothA: Usually one root; furcation can occur; distal depression common
comparisonRows.toothB: Usually one root and canal; distal depression remains common

comparisonRows.label: Ridge/groove clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Strong transverse ridge, no central groove, ML groove may occur
comparisonRows.toothB: H/U groove in two-cusp type or Y groove in three-cusp type

comparisonRows.label: Arch clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Canine-like transition tooth with strong lingual tilt
comparisonRows.toothB: More molar-like tooth with broader functional table

comparisonRows.label: Clinical identification clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Tiny lingual cusp plus transverse ridge
comparisonRows.toothB: Functional lingual cusp system plus recognizable groove pattern

questions.commonTrap: Do not mistake the mandibular first premolar’s separate pits for a short central groove; the transverse ridge interrupts that path.

sourceRefs.sourceName: REHE 151 Dental Anatomy Course Mastery Guide.pdf
REHE 151 Dental Anatomy and Occlusion Textbook Companion.pdf

questions.prompt: Which tooth is more likely to look molar-like because its lingual cusp system is larger and may split into two cusps?
questions.explanation: The mandibular second premolar has the broader, more functional occlusal table and may have a three-cusp form with separate mesiolingual and distolingual cusps.
questions.distractorExplanation: The mandibular first premolar is the canine-like transition tooth; its dominant facial cusp and tiny lingual cusp keep the table narrow and asymmetric.

comparisonRows.label: Crown shape
comparisonRows.toothA: Diamond outline with triangular occlusal table
comparisonRows.toothB: Ovoid two-cusp or squarer three-cusp outline

comparisonRows.label: Lingual anatomy
comparisonRows.toothA: Tiny pointed, often nonfunctional lingual cusp
comparisonRows.toothB: Larger functional lingual cusp or ML and DL cusps

comparisonRows.label: Root pattern
comparisonRows.toothA: Usually one root; furcation can occur; distal depression common
comparisonRows.toothB: Usually one root and canal; distal depression remains common

comparisonRows.label: Ridge/groove clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Strong transverse ridge, no central groove, ML groove may occur
comparisonRows.toothB: H/U groove in two-cusp type or Y groove in three-cusp type

comparisonRows.label: Arch clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Canine-like transition tooth with strong lingual tilt
comparisonRows.toothB: More molar-like tooth with broader functional table

comparisonRows.label: Clinical identification clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Tiny lingual cusp plus transverse ridge
comparisonRows.toothB: Functional lingual cusp system plus recognizable groove pattern

questions.commonTrap: The three-cusp second premolar is a common form, not the only form. The two-cusp type remains valid and uses H or U groove patterns.

sourceRefs.sourceName: REHE 151 Dental Anatomy Course Mastery Guide.pdf
REHE 151 Dental Anatomy and Occlusion Textbook Companion.pdf
da 5 Mand PM 2 2021 9 23 a.ppt

questions.prompt: Which molar more commonly shows reduced distolingual anatomy and may become a three-cusp, heart-shaped tooth?
questions.explanation: The maxillary second molar commonly reduces the distolingual cusp and talon; when that cusp is absent, the crown can take a three-cusp heart shape.
questions.distractorExplanation: The maxillary first molar is the larger reference molar with a fuller talon, four major cusps, and a stronger oblique ridge.

comparisonRows.label: Crown shape
comparisonRows.toothA: Large rhomboid with full trigon and talon
comparisonRows.toothB: Smaller twisted rhomboid or heart form when three-cusped

comparisonRows.label: Lingual anatomy
comparisonRows.toothA: Full ML and DL cusps; Carabelli may be present on ML and is variable
comparisonRows.toothB: DL cusp may be reduced or absent; Carabelli less likely

comparisonRows.label: Root pattern
comparisonRows.toothA: Three roots with broader separation
comparisonRows.toothB: Three roots that are closer and more parallel or swept distally

comparisonRows.label: Ridge/groove clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Strong ML-to-DB oblique ridge
comparisonRows.toothB: Oblique ridge retained in four-cusp form but altered with talon reduction

comparisonRows.label: Arch clue
comparisonRows.toothA: First-molar reference with full distal-lingual anatomy
comparisonRows.toothB: Distal progression simplifies the talon and narrows root spread

comparisonRows.label: Clinical identification clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Full talon and strong oblique ridge; variable Carabelli may support
comparisonRows.toothB: Reduced DL cusp plus converging roots

questions.commonTrap: A four-cusp maxillary second molar can still look rhomboid. Use size, talon reduction, and root convergence together.

sourceRefs.sourceName: REHE 151 Dental Anatomy Course Mastery Guide.pdf
REHE 151 Dental Anatomy and Occlusion Textbook Companion.pdf
da 6 Max M1 2024.ppt
da 6 Max M2 M3 2020.ppt

questions.prompt: Which molar is the stronger match for three roots that sit closer together and sweep more parallel?
questions.explanation: Both teeth use the maxillary three-root plan, but the second molar’s roots are typically closer together and more parallel, with a longer root trunk.
questions.distractorExplanation: The first molar’s three roots are more widely spread and support its fuller crown form; root convergence is the second-molar clue.

comparisonRows.label: Crown shape
comparisonRows.toothA: Large rhomboid with full trigon and talon
comparisonRows.toothB: Smaller twisted rhomboid or heart form when three-cusped

comparisonRows.label: Lingual anatomy
comparisonRows.toothA: Full ML and DL cusps; Carabelli may be present on ML and is variable
comparisonRows.toothB: DL cusp may be reduced or absent; Carabelli less likely

comparisonRows.label: Root pattern
comparisonRows.toothA: Three roots with broader separation
comparisonRows.toothB: Three roots that are closer and more parallel or swept distally

comparisonRows.label: Ridge/groove clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Strong ML-to-DB oblique ridge
comparisonRows.toothB: Oblique ridge retained in four-cusp form but altered with talon reduction

comparisonRows.label: Arch clue
comparisonRows.toothA: First-molar reference with full distal-lingual anatomy
comparisonRows.toothB: Distal progression simplifies the talon and narrows root spread

comparisonRows.label: Clinical identification clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Full talon and strong oblique ridge; variable Carabelli may support
comparisonRows.toothB: Reduced DL cusp plus converging roots

questions.commonTrap: Do not use root count alone; both are commonly three-rooted. Root spacing and talon form do the separating.

sourceRefs.sourceName: REHE 151 Dental Anatomy Course Mastery Guide.pdf

da 6 Max M2 M3 2020.ppt

questions.prompt: Which tooth is the classic four-cusp, rectangular mandibular molar with a plus-shaped groove pattern?
questions.explanation: The mandibular second molar is the regular four-cusp tooth with a rectangular outline and plus-shaped groove system.
questions.distractorExplanation: The mandibular first molar is usually pentagonal with five cusps and a Y-pattern groove system created in part by its distal cusp.

comparisonRows.label: Crown shape
comparisonRows.toothA: Pentagonal, longest mesiodistal crown, usually five cusps
comparisonRows.toothB: More regular rectangular crown with four cusps

comparisonRows.label: Lingual anatomy
comparisonRows.toothA: ML and DL cusps with stronger lingual taper; distal cusp may show
comparisonRows.toothB: ML and DL cusps align in a more regular four-cusp plan

comparisonRows.label: Root pattern
comparisonRows.toothA: Two roots with wide separation and shorter trunk
comparisonRows.toothB: Two roots closer together and more parallel

comparisonRows.label: Ridge/groove clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Y-pattern groove system
comparisonRows.toothB: Plus-pattern groove system with two transverse ridges

comparisonRows.label: Arch clue
comparisonRows.toothA: First molar carries the distal cusp and greatest MD width
comparisonRows.toothB: Second molar simplifies into a symmetric four-cusp block

comparisonRows.label: Clinical identification clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Five cusps plus Y pattern and broad root spread
comparisonRows.toothB: Four cusps plus pattern and close parallel roots

questions.commonTrap: Cusp count can vary. Confirm the groove pattern, outline, and root spacing before naming the tooth.

questions.prompt: Which tooth is identified most reliably by the combined bundle of five cusps, Y grooves, and widely separated roots?
questions.explanation: The mandibular first molar’s strongest identification bundle is five cusps, a pentagonal outline with a Y groove pattern, and two widely separated roots.
questions.distractorExplanation: The mandibular second molar is the more symmetric four-cusp, rectangular, plus-pattern tooth, and its roots lie closer together.

comparisonRows.label: Crown shape
comparisonRows.toothA: Pentagonal, longest mesiodistal crown, usually five cusps
comparisonRows.toothB: More regular rectangular crown with four cusps

comparisonRows.label: Lingual anatomy
comparisonRows.toothA: ML and DL cusps with stronger lingual taper; distal cusp may show
comparisonRows.toothB: ML and DL cusps align in a more regular four-cusp plan

comparisonRows.label: Root pattern
comparisonRows.toothA: Two roots with wide separation and shorter trunk
comparisonRows.toothB: Two roots closer together and more parallel

comparisonRows.label: Ridge/groove clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Y-pattern groove system
comparisonRows.toothB: Plus-pattern groove system with two transverse ridges

comparisonRows.label: Arch clue
comparisonRows.toothA: First molar carries the distal cusp and greatest MD width
comparisonRows.toothB: Second molar simplifies into a symmetric four-cusp block

comparisonRows.label: Clinical identification clue
comparisonRows.toothA: Five cusps plus Y pattern and broad root spread
comparisonRows.toothB: Four cusps plus pattern and close parallel roots

questions.commonTrap: A mandibular first molar can vary to four or six cusps. The identification bundle is stronger than cusp count by itself.
